raw_payload.author: noodojani
raw_payload.content: I have seen this movie and I feel like it's somewhere between average two and OK movie I simply don't understand the praise is the critics are giving and now 16 Oscar nominations this movie at no point is at the level of Titanic inception Godfather or any of these highly rated Oscar nominated movies. I simply don't understand the disconnect between the actual movie and the praise it's getting maybe I am wrong but I do feel like Oscar needs to remain impartial and unbiased at that give out nomination simply because they feel sorry for a particular demographic.

raw_payload.author: SugarCottoncandy
raw_payload.content:
A film that adds nothing from an artistic and cinematographic point of view.

First part of a deadly boredom, second based on clichés, derivative and poorly used narrative elements.

A less than mediocre blockbuster.

Yet another case of a film exalted by obsolete criticism and an audience poorly educated to the true refinement of the seventh art.

The fact that in May it's considered the best film of the year would make one believe in a mass hallucination but unfortunately the acclaim and success it has had is only the mirror of the times: a lack of truly worthy creativity and an increasingly poor and faded appreciation index.

raw_payload.author: MadMark96
raw_payload.content:
When I reluctantly sat down to watch this I was expecting it to be another overrated horror movie praised by people who have only thinly scratched the surface of the genre but what I saw instead was an actually fairly unique story with a typical vampire plot as the backdrop.

The score that's been praised by many other viewers I'll have to agree was very good and were indeed some of the highest points of the movie.

In the age of films that rely on a more natural look for lighting rather than a specific color grading, Sinners stands out particularly and I really enjoyed the coloring of the film throughout and it gave it a really distinct vibe and overall it's shot much more uniquely than a lot of other box office heavy hitters have been.

The acting was good throughout, Michael B Jordan of course puts on a good performance as usual (even in his worst rated movies). The supporting cast was great with a really good villain and the folklore is nicely woven into the story. Learning more about the mythology of the communities the characters come from was really engaging and I would love to see this universe explored more.

Without spoiling any of the best scenes of the film, there is a really cool underlying theme of transcending generational boundaries with deep connections to ancestry that really add to the subtext of the film.

I appreciate the option to go for carefully crafted tension over worn out jump scares and there were genuinely some crazy moments in the movie. There were many points I was expecting a dumb jump scare noise to blare in my ears and it didn't happen and instead relied on the genuine eeriness of the scenes which was another pleasant surprise.

The special effects on the vampires look great for the most part especially on the hands, although I am not a fan of the way they did the red eyes though. They look very computer generated and fake in some of the lighting.

I've seen many viewers drawing comparisons of the directors work to Jordan Peele and while there are certainly some similarities, Sinners runs laps around any film in Peele's catalogue. Including in the department of racial commentary.

Some very minor pacing issues also prevent me from giving it a higher score. The most noticeable for me personally was the big showdown with the vampires wraps up a smidge too sloppy and quick. I think in a movie that was very creative with a lot of its lore and universe it could've done this a bit better but it was still entertaining nonetheless.

If you like vampire movies or looking for a good new monster movie with a unique flair definitely check it out.

raw_payload.author: jared-25331
raw_payload.content:
Sinners (2025) is the latest offering from writer-director Ryan Coogler, the first horror movie he has made and it is by far his best movie to date.

Positives for Sinners (2025): This is a good old fashion vampire movie at its finest with some added details to the plot and story. You have a fun set of characters protrayed by a phenomenal cast of actors. Everyone in the cast is having the time of their lives especially Michael B. Jordan and Hailee Steinfeld. Aside from that, this is just an expertly directed movie from start to finish. The cinematography looks gorgeous and there are a lot of amazing shots in the movie. The use of music is also phenomenal and it plays a major part in the movie. There are buckets of blood that will satisfy gore hounds like me. I also loved that Ryan Coogler used practical effects for the gore in the movie and that makes me more happy as a horror fan. There is also social commentary on culture and racism that is done very well and in a way that feels natural and organic. The musical score from Ludwig Goransson is perfect with everything happening on screen. And finally, the ending to this movie is absolutely perfect in every way possible.

Overall, Sinners (2025) is an insanely phenomenal ride that proves to Hollywood that you can make a great original movie with a big budget and a great cast. The is by far my favorite movie of 2025 and we still have a stacked list for the rest of the year.

raw_payload.author: MsH-185
raw_payload.content:
Went to see this because I like vampire movies, and Tom Cruise endorsed it. Never saw him endorsing any movies so I got more curious.

Production, costume, make up, visuals are very good.

Story is meh. Standard vampire look and behavior. Another movie implying some faith doesn't work in fighting demons but a voodoo mojo does, nothing new there.

I dislike musicals so its musical parts added nothing for me. The past and future music evoked could have been more abstract, but they were real people in different eras costumes mingling in, kinda ruined the believability of the 1930s vibe for me. Looked like a silly company dinner and dance instead.

Can totally see certain demographic overhyping this. Again, nothing new.

An okay watch overall, fun at times.

raw_payload.author: meaeo
raw_payload.content:
Excellent movie with strong performances from everyone in the cast. Instead of remakes and comic books, finally an original story with heart and creativity. The music is amazing, and I loved the visuals.

To prove the point about there not being small parts, every cast member plays a key role in moving the story forward. The only spoiler here is my wish that Cornbread would have made it - but again, every act was integral to the outcome.

I've watched this twice now, and it's one of the rare movies I would willing watch multiple times because it is just that great. The best is hearing from others I've recommended it to also coming back and expressing their admiration for the movie and recommending to others.

raw_payload.author: MamadNobari97
raw_payload.content:
Once again, I'm going into a film praised and hyped by pretty much everyone, expecting to experience some greatness, but in the end, all I got was mid.

Now I can see why people like this; it's not a bad movie, and it's fairly entertaining.

It also has a lot, and I mean a lot of underlying (or rather overt) themes and metaphors that some are shoved into your face, which make the movie more than just another action vampire flick.

But what I don't understand is why people overhype it as the best movie that's come out in 30 years or the best movie ever made. At least with some of the famous top-rated movies, even if you don't like them, you can still see why people think they are important and are the best of the best. But Sinners is just meh. I guess there have been so many bad and mid movies recently that a movie that's competently made, which would be considered mid any other year among many more good films, is considered amazing for some reason.

But on the other hand, I think this movie's plot and its themes are all over the place.

The plot is very thin, and the movie is character-focused. But the problem is, if I'm watching a character-driven movie, I expect to actually sympathize with them and have an emotional connection to them. If I don't care about the characters and there's not much of a plot, really, then what's the point? I'm just watching another vampire flick that doesn't leave a lasting impression on me to remember it distinctly from other vampire flicks.

Now, the movie visually looks great, the cinematography is good, and the production is great. The sound design is also great. Some of the action scenes also look great.

But apart from that, the movie is really cliché-ridden, and the pacing doesn't really help the film either.

Overall, it's not a bad movie at all, it's well-made, the acting is pretty decent, looks great, has some great shots, has some very heavy themes so it's not just a dumb action film, but it is ultimately disappointing and not memorable for me, way too overhyped and overpraised.

raw_payload.author: nishamdorsey
raw_payload.content: I really enjoyed Sinners! Not a perfect movie but definitely a good one. It's giving "cult classic" vibes for me. We don't get a lot of original content nowadays that isn't already tied to a franchise or existing IP so thank you Ryan Coogler for creating something artful with a story of its own. Admittedly, the protagonist "Preacher Boy" sparked immediate comparisons for me to urban legend blues man archetypes like Robert Johnson but stands out in how this character's story is told. It feels personal. It feels inspired and I was along for the ride. For me, Sinners is the live action version of an Ernie Barnes painting, particularly "The Sugar Shack", and I am so here for bringing back such depictions of Black life and art. There is a powerfully moving juke joint scene that motivates the darker themes of the film while simultaneously shining in pushing both narrative and nostalgia. It weaves ancestry and anarchy masterfully. And it proves why we need more black creators in the supernatural horror genre! Michael B. Jordan is getting the well deserved attention for his performances but I would argue to say that this is an ensemble effort where everyone sunk their teeth into their respective roles. Final thought: If From Dusk Till Dawn, Idlewild, & Eve's Bayou had a one night stand they would conceive Twins called Smoke & Stacks.

raw_payload.author: Movie_Rating_n_Ranking
raw_payload.content:
Es, there's no doubt about it. Sinners is one of the best films of 2025, but I hesitate to put it at number 1, because while there are truly spectacular elements, there are also others that I can't stop looking at sideways. Let's start with the good stuff. Direction, photography, editing, and soundtrack. Impeccable. At a level of excellence. I love the choice of camera lenses for many of the shots; I like the play of lighting and also the lack of it to include it within the plot itself; I like the warm colors used in the color palette; I like that the editing has clever and fun transitions, and that they're not just dry cuts; and above all, I love the soundtrack, full of blues, rock, and folk.

Now, let's move on to the interesting part: the script. I can't help but think that Sinners seems like a pair of very different films. The first half is a period piece, with gangsters and some drama. The second half is horror and action. They're like the two hemispheres of the brain: part of a single concept, but very different from each other. If Coogler had better distributed the film's elements throughout, perhaps Sinners would have been an unquestionable masterpiece. Now, let there be no doubt, both halves are very well done in every way. The film is really good. The only thing that bothers me is the way the story was put together.

Ryan Coogler's latest installment makes it into my top three vampire films and remains the great recommendation for 2025.

raw_payload.author: roens_reviews
raw_payload.content:
Don't buy into the hype. Sinners is a slightly above average film that has been blown massively out of proportion. Its current rating of an 8.1/10 is absurdly high. It's not THAT good.

I'll start with the positives. First of all, I thought the acting was absolutely amazing. It was a really fantastic performance from the entire cast. I also thought the music was great; l'm not typically much of a blues guy, but l enjoyed what I heard.

As for the negatives, the biggest problem I had is that the film accomplished nothing. It finished and I was just like.....ok? What am I to make of this? It's categorized as Drama and Supernatural Horror, and while it is technically both of those, I wasn't impressed in either area. Suspense--a major part of both of those genres--was basically nonexistent. The 'normal' part of the plot (the stuff without the supernatural junk) was actually solid. It had a nice little twist in it, which the ending went back on. I just wish there was more of that.

Overall, it wasn't a bad movie, but it's not one I'd recommend. The best way to sum it up is this: it's a dime a dozen horror flick that's made watchable by a really good cast and great cinematography.

Final score of 6/10.

raw_payload.author: melvindeondavis
raw_payload.content:
I like Michael B Jordan and Ryan Coogler, but this one boring movie. Like my friend said, it's pretty much Dusk To Dawn "revamped".

Most of the movie will put you to sleep. A few funny scenes and sayings from back in the day kept it interesting and that's about it. I'm glad I didn't go to the theater to see this. Hopefully they won't try to make a sequel or a prequel. I've seen all of the Dusk To Dawn movies already...

raw_payload.author: Exorcistus
raw_payload.content:
It really felt like the director, Coogler, just gave up on his own project before it was even finished.

The talking and dialogue in the movie was bad. A lot of times, I couldn't hear what they were saying, and when I could, it didn't make much sense.

The story itself was also weak and non-engaging, and the way they edited and put the movie together was messy and sloppy. Nothing really seemed to connect nor made sense.

It looked like Coogler was trying to mix movies like "Inglourious Basterds" (instead of killing nazis, they were killing KKK) and "From Dusk till Dawn," (yeah, let's change to vampires in the middle of the movie) but it just didn't work at all, it super-misfired on all cylinders.

There were also some obvious newbie mistakes. For example, there's a scene with the twins standing next to each other, but one of their shadows was super long and the other was short. It was clear those parts were filmed at different times of the day. The director didn't seem to care about these details.

Something else that really bothered me was that even after everyone knew the bad guys were vampires, the vampires kept using the same dumb lines and acting innocent to try and get inside. Even a little kid wouldn't fall for that.

Basically, "Sinners" felt rushed and not well-made at all. Zero enjoyment out of 2 hours wasted.

Makes me believe that many of the high ratings are probably bought and fake.

raw_payload.author: Rashesh231
raw_payload.content:
We'll talk about the film, but let me start with the trailer first. Only when the makers have absolute confidence in their film can they craft such a stunning trailer. It shows a lot, yet reveals nothing. I'd still prefer that one doesn't watch the trailers, especially the 2nd one before watching the film. You'll enjoy the film even more if you don't.

Now, coming to the film. What a beauty! The performances and screenplay are absolutely on point. Michael B. Jordan delivers a career-high performance in his double role as twin brothers Smoke and Stack. Miles Caton as Sammie, the blues guitarist, steals every scene he's in with a soulful, electric presence. Everyone else is great in their roles too.

The film pulls you into its universe, and you become completely immersed. It builds up gradually, with the first hour unfolding as a deliberate, blues-infused drama that just carefully sets up its characters and the theme. Once it hits its stride and the horror kicks in, there is no looking back.

Around the halfway mark, you begin to understand what's really happening. And once the plot unfolds, you are taken on a blend of intrigue, thrill, anxiety, and (some) humour. You just can't take your eyes off the screen. It's epic!

The music too does not just accompany the story, it elevates it and becomes a part of its soul. There's one jaw-dropping sequence, and it is pure movie magic, bridging past and future through music in probably one of the best cinematic moments ever witnessed in a film.

For me, Sinners for sure is the best film of 2025 so far. I just loved everything about it. It starts slow but steadily begins to surprise and amaze. Every next 10 minutes feels better than the last, and it just keeps going higher from there.

It has all the flavours of a cult classic, one that would be talked about for years to come.

raw_payload.author: DrGadimov
raw_payload.content: Sinners (2025) blew me away-it's this wild vampire flick set in 1930s Mississippi during Jim Crow, mixing horror with Southern Gothic drama and killer Blues music that feels like straight-up magic. Michael B. Jordan plays twin brothers, one a haunted pianist, the other a tough soldier, coming home to start a juke joint but unleashing Irish vamps who offer "easy freedom" by sucking souls. The visuals are gorgeous-gritty swamps, lush IMAX shots-and Ludwig Göransson's score with Blues riffs is next-level, tying into African roots and community rituals against the Klan's real terror. Themes hit hard on oppression, memory, and true vs. Fake liberation. Only gripe: first half's a slow period drama burn, then it flips to vampire chaos and ends with a Klan showdown-like three movies jammed in. Still, Jordan's performance is insane (thought they were different actors!). 8/10-totally rewatchable, super original. If you dig smart horror with heart, run to it!

raw_payload.author: rebecca-47281
raw_payload.content:
The acting and cinematography were excellent, and Coogler's setting and casting choices were top-notch. However, I found myself increasingly bored as the film progressed, especially once the white characters (Remmick etc.) arrive at the house.

At that point, the vampire metaphor for cultural appropriation and exploitation of Black culture felt obvious and heavy-handed. More like a literary cliche than the powerful statement it aimed to be. Coogler's message is correct, but the execution sacrificed plot momentum and suspense entirely. The film abandoned its narrative tension in favor of hammering home its political message. It ultimately devolved into a splatter film where the social commentary overshadowed any remaining storytelling.

raw_payload.author: freq32
raw_payload.content: At the core of this movie is southern blues music from the 1930s. It's a period piece from 1932 Mississippi with particularly memorable performances from Michael B Jordan, Jack O'Connel, Delroy Lindo, Wunmi Mosaku and Miles Caton (great blues singing). If you love blues music with a twist of campy horror (yeah weird right), this may actually be your thing. I happened to really enjoy it because the script really tied everything together without any loose ends in the end that make you question it. Vampires? Some seemed turned off by the vampire element but I was into it. Pay attention to Annie and the guitar for clues on how that all came about. It's subtle, but there. I noticed it. Absolutely recommend.

raw_payload.author: mariannoh
raw_payload.content:
This is, by far, the weirdest film I have ever seen! But, at the same time it is so freakin' fascinating, I cannot help but liking it. A lot. It just tickles my fancy, and I cannot even tell you why. And maybe that IS why? I honestly dunno, but it sure is worth the time. You will be surprised, you will be confused, you will ask yourself what the devil you are watching... But in the end, you will be left with a feeling of having watched something unique and worthwhile.

The ending was disappointing though, to be honest. But still, all in all, a solid 8/10.

raw_payload.author: shankysodhi
raw_payload.content:
I challenge you all claiming it to be a masterpiece to give me another example of a masterpiece movie you are comparing it with.

Are you all really that shallow and bored you will give any movie that title.

As a drama movie I will give it a 7.

As a horror not more than a 6.

There are maybe 2-3 jump scares. And the story is half decent. It. Could have been used to build a great movie. But there is none of that. Maybe the blues fans will enjoy a song or two but that's it. Too many characters introduced, very little development on each front. Sets are decent.

Don't listen to all bought reviews online.

raw_payload.author: DesireeS-6
raw_payload.content:
I went into Sinners not knowing what to expect, and honestly, it blew me away. This isn't just another drama-it's the kind of film that reaches into something real and raw, the kind that lingers in your mind and leaves you thinking long after it ends.

The story is layered and deeply human, tackling guilt, redemption, and forgiveness with a level of honesty that feels rare. Nothing is sugarcoated, and yet there's this underlying hope that pulls everything together. The characters are flawed in the most relatable way, and you genuinely feel for them.

Michael B. Jordan delivered something that's subtle yet incredibly powerful-you can read an entire backstory just in a look. The supporting cast is just as compelling, and together they make every emotional beat hit exactly as it should.

Cinematography, music, direction-everything works in harmony. It's not overproduced or showy, but you can tell every detail was handled with intention. The atmosphere it creates is haunting, beautiful, and intimate.

Sinners is the kind of movie that reminds you why storytelling matters. It's about brokenness, yes, but also about healing and hope. It's rare to find a film this grounded, this moving, and this honest.

One of the best films I've seen in a long time!

raw_payload.author: Terrorbit
raw_payload.content: Set in 1930s Mississippi, Ryan Coogler's film combines vampire lore, racial drama and musical western to create an ambitious hybrid. The story serves as a metaphor for structural racism and cultural exploitation, with the blues functioning not only as a soundtrack but also as a symbol of collective resistance. Shot on 65mm and visually stunning, the film is characterised by a stylised aesthetic and dense, eerie atmospheres. However, despite a strong cast and some compelling moments, the accumulation of themes - from historical trauma to redemption and black identity - ultimately overwhelms the narrative, which becomes muddled and inconsistent. Violence and horror come too late to really make an impact, and some symbolic ideas, like the "inclusive" white vampires or the presence of the Choctaws, feel underdeveloped. Coogler aims high, but doesn't always hit the mark: the film stands out more for what it hints at than what it has to say. The result is a bold but uneven work that favors discourse rather than suspense and focuses more on thesis than emotional resonance.

raw_payload.author: courtneypedersen
raw_payload.content:
I really wanted to love this movie. The previews made it look like it was going to be an amazing action/horror movie which I'm always down for.

The movie started off strong and I was enjoying it. It had the vibe of Lawless and I know the first half was setting up the characters so the audience would care about the, but after they get to the juke joint, it goes down hill quickly!

This movie clearly could not decide what it wanted to be: drama, action, horror, musical. The musical part of it drove me insane. The Blues sequence made sense and I really enjoyed it. But why was there a second musical sequence/performance??

The whole movie had a very disjointed feeling, the scenes weren't really tied together and the things the various characters did made no sense. By the time people started turning into vampires, I didn't care about them anymore. I was actually looking forward to them dying because that would mean I would be done with the movie that much sooner.

Honestly, they had good ideas for 3 separate movies: one for the first half where we get to see the twins life. Two, the musical sequences could've been part of a really good musical. Three, the vampire and horror aspect. If these three ideas were taken and developed separately, that would've been better.

As it is, I don't understand how this movie is so popular and loved. I also don't understand how this movie has managed to be nominated for the most Oscar awards ever.

raw_payload.author: SnoopyStyle
raw_payload.content:
In 1932, Smoke (Michael B. Jordan) and Stack Moore (Michael B. Jordan) return home to Clarksdale, Mississippi to open a juke joint. They are WWI veterans who worked in the shady underworld of gangland Chicago. They are joined by their young nephew Sammie Moore (Miles Caton). Mary (Hailee Steinfeld) is a mixed race girl from their past. Delta Slim (Delroy Lindo) is a drunken piano player. Smoke's estranged wife Annie is practitioner of the magical arts. As the party gets going, three white musicians show up at their door.

Ryan Coogler has crammed so much into his film. It is magical realism, the music, the location, the times, the race politics, and the amazing music. I did wonder where we are going with this in the first forty minutes. I am delightfully surprised by the Chinese couple having two different shops in the segregated world. From the music, I thought of a deal with the devil. From the times, I thought of the KKK. I go into this relatively blind and the turn is exhilarating. This is an intoxicating mix of materials and may be the best movie I've seen this year.

raw_payload.author: christineeldred
raw_payload.content: O Brother, Where Art Thou? Meets From Dusk Til Dawn! Beautifully filmed in a graphic comic book style. The music is an eclectic collection of old blues, bluegrass and old Irish ballads. This movie is set in 1932 in Mississippi. Is it horror? Yes! I appreciate these types of movies that don't spoon feed you art. I like that it took chances even if it makes some people uncomfortable. Deal with it I say. America has a history and I enjoy the fact that it wasn't glossed over but a part of the story. The acting is well done by the cast. If you want an entertaining movie and you're a musical nerd like myself who appreciates music history this is a fun one.

raw_payload.author: aird_kris
raw_payload.content: I hope this isn't a spoiler but I'm sure you've seen the trailer...It's a "From Dusk till Dawn" ripoff that is looking for a new audience. There's no twists here. It's redeeming feature is it's time setting, 1930's, but once you get into the "bar" it's a genre piece with no new content. Using the same actor in "twin" roles has been done before and better. The rest of the casts' acting is solid enough but it runs out of steam well before it's 137min running time does. I saw it at the cinema and the soundscape was solid enough but that's not sufficiently engrossing to keep you anywhere near the edge of your seat. I've given it a six star rating which us possibly a bit generous, but we all love vampire movies don't we, even if there are more of them in films and series than us actually watching them these days. It's fun but it's nothing new to the genre so don't go in with high expectations, and you'll not be let down. I wasn't looking at my watch but I would have missed nothing if I left to get some popcorn. My brother gave it a 7, but he's a generous chap. It'll be streaming somewhere soon so don't rush out but if you wanna go to the movies, you could definitely do a lot worse.

raw_payload.author: Coralielmn
raw_payload.content:
Sinners was a real surprise. The beginning is slow, almost contemplative, but I quickly realized that the pacing was intentional. The film takes its time to build atmosphere, to let the characters and silences speak.

What struck me the most was the visual beauty: every shot feels carefully composed, almost painterly. There's a real sense of control in the direction. The aesthetic is refined without being pretentious, always in service of the story and emotion.

The soundtrack is another major strength. It doesn't just accompany the scenes, it elevates them. It helped me fully sink into this dark, melancholic, sometimes unsettling world.

I was also genuinely moved by the performances of Hailee Steinfeld and Michael B. Jordan. Their chemistry works beautifully, fitting the film's atmosphere perfectly.

A beautiful cinematic experience.

raw_payload.author: tylerchrestman
raw_payload.content: Sinners has some issues. This movie is trying so hard to be From Dusk Till Dawn, but it just comes up short in comparison. The first act involves Michael B. Jordan playing a set of twins as they set up for a party, it's slow and not very interesting. I get technology is getting better, but I don't like one actor playing two sides of a conversation. It's not the Nutty Professor, it never feels fluid, and it's probably why the two main characters spend most of the movie apart. Sinners struggles to get going but once the party starts you think you're in store for some cool vampire fight scenes, but you are mistaken. Instead, vampires dance an Irish Jig that caused the person I was watching the movie with to lean over and say to me "this is the dumbest thing I've ever seen." what it didn't have was some cool vampire fights. The action scenes are weak and for a movie that's also weak on character development and plot, you can't afford to also be lackluster with the action. This movie would probably be more fun if you went in blind and didn't know about the genre switch coming your way, but it ruins that with too much foreshadowing early on. Overall, I don't get what all the hype is about 6/10.

raw_payload.author: Jalitzual
raw_payload.content:
An absolute masterclass of directing and acting on display here. It's just so cool to see to Micheal B Jordan's and how different they are to each other as well, very nicely done. Truly, every actor holds there own, and are captivating to watch. It was also exciting to see new talent on the big screen, and I'm sure we'll see this cast in many more things to come.

Now this is definitely a vampire movie and a fun, and spooky one at that but there's also deep and beautiful messaging here about religion and music and that I think that'll be audiences biggest takeaway. Last thing, there's a sequence in the middle of this movie, and you'll know the one and it is simply incredible. Honestly the best movie of 2025 so far, go watch it!

raw_payload.author: lewisv3919
raw_payload.content:
I tried to understand why so many people were raving about this movie. I will admit that the cinematic experience was great. Some really beautiful scenes that took me by surprise. The costume were fitting for the era. Everything about in every scene was beautiful. But where it lost it's greatness to me was not the acting, but in it's trying to convince me that these vampires was menacing. The eyes were just too comical for me and the gore was too over the top. One area where it hit hard was the music. Even if you are not a fan of the genre you will enjoy the music.

That is something very special that we don't see often in the movies, using music in this was to tell a story.

raw_payload.author: anishgb
raw_payload.content:
I absolutely loved this movie. What immediately struck me was how perfectly the period setting was realized. It didn't just feel like a backdrop; it was immersive and atmospheric, really pulling me into that specific time and place.

But the real heart of the film lies in its wonderful characters. They were so richly developed and complex that I quickly became completely invested in their stories and fates. Michael B Jordan was absolutely fantastic in his dual role of Smoke and Stack,

What I also found truly brilliant was the unique way the filmmakers blended two completely different genres: an excellent mix of blues music and vampire folklore. The music wasn't just score; it was integral to the story, giving the vampire mythology a soulful, mournful, and incredibly cool twist I've never seen before.

When the credits rolled, I felt a deep, powerful sense of satisfaction, but also a poignant sadness-it was a genuinely bittersweet experience. It's the kind of movie that stays with you long after you've watched it. I highly recommend it.

raw_payload.author: shinrisen
raw_payload.content:
Sinners was a flick I was excited to see, mostly due to it being recommended by many (both off and online) and due to the high ratings it's been getting. However, I'm sad to say it didn't live up to my expectations.

The film has a terrific bunch of characters, better than 'From Dusk Till Dawn', the only film I can somewhat compare it to. The first half of the movie is mostly a "get the team together" part, much in the vein of "Blues Brothers" or heck, "Ocean's Eleven". Part of the problem is that, while the film has strong character building in this first half, not a lot actually happens. I was preconditioned with the knowledge that it had been called a spin on 'From Dusk Till Dawn', so when it is laid out that Smoke and Stack (both portrayed by Michael B. Jordan, who does a good job) are organizing a 'juke joint' at some barn, it was obvious this would be the 'horror' part of the film. So the main plot point of the first hour or so is just that; there's going to be a party later.

When the party actually starts, it has the most interesting/creative scene of the film; in which 'Preacherboy' (Miles Caton) performs his delta blues song, and the scene is juxtaposed with musicians from others times/regions. I was hoping the film would do more with that theme, but alas, it's just that one scene. Speaking of which, there's a lot of music in the film, and while I like blues music, it sometimes held up the pace of events. There's ways to incorporate music in films which enhances events in the film, but here the musical acts were too front and center in my opinion.

Shortly after that, we get to the "characters making dumb decisions" part, which seems to plague a lot of horror films. The Smokestack brothers seem to figure out that organizing juke joints for poor cotton farmers in the local area isn't very lucrative (who would've guessed?) and thus one of the brothers sends his love interest (Mary, played by Hailee Steinfeld) to get three 'white folk' who had been turned away previously due to being creepy (and white, which one of the brothers point out can be recipe for disaster in 1930's Jim Crowe's America). Why? Are these three people going to turn the night profitable? At that point Mary gets turned into a vampire and pretty soon all hell breaks loose. Stack gets bitten by her, the party stops, everyone leaves, everyone gets turned into vampires and we got our classic Assault on Precinct 13 scenario, in which a small group of hero's battle it out against evil.

A lot of dumb decisions are made during the buildup to the final battle, in which one of the dumb characters decides to invite all the vampires in about 30 minutes before the sun comes up. The battle itself is choreographed decently, but a lot of plot armor is dished around, a lot of cliché's play out (another love interest who foreshadowed that, if she gets bitten, Stack should finish her off before she turns, which plays out inevitably as planned). Preacherboy tries to escape, gets caught by the main vampire who proceeds to talk to him for 15 minutes with his fangs out, then gets bashed in the head while the suns gets up. Bleh.

Then we get a scene in which Stack (who also survived) shoots up some KKK members, because why not. At least in 'Django', shooting up the Klan felt earned. Here it seemed just another box to tick off.

Now I've only described the film on its surface elements. There's obviously a lot of subtext in terms of the vampires, who seem to share each other's memories and the main vampire who seems focussed on getting Preacherboy (who seems to be a brilliant Blues musician). So I suppose one could argue that Preacherboy is an metaphor for 'black music/culture', and the vampires are perhaps greed/capitalism, who want to suck the lifeblood out of the culture and homogenize it and turn it in an instrument for its own purpose? I'd have to think on that some more. The main vampire seems to be Irish and is hunted earlier in the film by native Americans who get turned away by some KKK members. Probably a lot more subtext that I missed.

But in short; the film tries to bite off more than it can chew, doesn't really know what it wants to do, and thus felt unsatisfying on about every front. It's well produced in terms of cinematography, costumes/production design and has some good characters, but that's about all the praise I can give it. It's also too long, I feel it could've shaved of at least 20 minutes. It wasn't terribly bad, but I wasn't entertained either.

6/10.

raw_payload.author: lorraineesimpson
raw_payload.content:
Heard so much about this movie. Was expecting something amazing and it's definitely not that. At best it's ok. The music is great and it's quite an original approach and that got it the 5 stars. However it's a flimsy story, there's an overly long introduction to the characters and I still couldn't have cared less about any of them. The horror bits are standard vampire stuff and end up as B movie nonsense. It's all completely forgettable and in no way a classic.

How this has got 16 nominations is beyond me. If it wins best picture that is surely a damning indictment of the rest. These movie critics who have been singing it's praises need to revisit their job descriptions!

raw_payload.author: timothy-625
raw_payload.content:
Almost impossible to enjoy because of how poorly it was exposed. The lighting was so dark that even in a pitch-black movie theater, I could barely make out what was happening on screen. Scenes that were supposed to be tense or dramatic lost all impact because I literally couldn't see anything.

It's frustrating when a film's technical choices prevent you from engaging with the story, and unfortunately, that's exactly what happened here. Even if the performances or plot might have had potential, the visual issues made it nearly unwatchable.

raw_payload.author: ronald-59
raw_payload.content:
It is a good movie but the music and sound make it very good.

Don't watch it if you don't have a proper sound system. Buy one or visit a friend that has one.

Take a good seat and relax. Watch the story getting alive. It is a story of two men and a musician living their lives and getting into trouble with a twist. Beautiful shots, beautiful music and good acting.

raw_payload.author: Fella_shibby
raw_payload.content:
This movie has everything, drama, action, music n horror but the best part is the character development.

Michael Jordan gave the twin characters their own individual quirks and mannerisms to make them feel like different people while still being twins.

The atmosphere n cinematography is awesome.

The dense southern set up filled with lush green trees, sultry cotton fields, shimmering heat, pulsating neon lights in the Juke joint n the tension of the Jim Crowe era done very well.

It immerses you in the ambiance of a 1930s rural juke joint, and we all want to be there but at the same time if we wud have known about the impending danger of the vampires n the bigots klux klan, we wud have second thots bah it.

The first thing will come to mind is From Dawn till Dusk but this shud not deter serious horror fans as this movie ain't at all comedic but intense horror.

I loved the end action sequence wher Jordan's character goes all gun blazing at the Klux Klan.

raw_payload.author: markhommerson
raw_payload.content: A vampire flick set in the blues-soaked south, just after the civil war. If that juxtaposition blows you away, which it really shouldn't, then this is your masterpiece. It hardly treads on new ground, with From Dusk till Dawn or Cowboys & Aliens in mind, and the high praise does not feel earned. I am saddened to see it is another movie that got pushed not through its own merits, more because of the racial background of the creators. That said, it isn't bad either. It will do. It is a stale cup of tea without a biscuit. Michael B. Jordan puts in a solid performance. And the movie does not overstay its welcome.

raw_payload.author: bsmiracle
raw_payload.content:
I am not a fan of scary movies EXCEPT vampire movies.. (I'm a chicken.) This movie is one of the best in the genre I have ever seen but it's not a 9 for vampire movie. It's a 9 because of the ability blend themes, experiences, music and threats so interchangeably despite there being so many different of each category... it's so smooth. Very talented cast from direction to extras.

Remick is a very cool antagonist- he was prob one of my least fav characters but it was not due to fault of actor would have loved more character development maybe flashbacks or something. There is character development I don't want to mislead just could have appreciated more.

raw_payload.author: lienneirynck
raw_payload.content:
Sinner's Bloodlines of the South definitely has potential. The eerie atmosphere of the American South mixed with supernatural elements sounds like a compelling combination on paper. Unfortunately, the execution falls short.

The sudden appearance of a vampire feels abrupt and unexplained, making it seem more like a random twist than an integrated part of the story. Key questions remain unanswered: Why is the vampire here? What is his connection to the main characters? And what role does music play, which seems important but never truly finds its place in the narrative?

The film attempts to be profound but struggles to develop its themes-such as ancestral guilt, identity, and the power of music-in a meaningful way. While there are some visually striking moments and the mood occasionally works, the writing lacks clarity and focus. With better exposition, character development, and buildup, this could have been a memorable experience.

Conclusion: A film full of atmosphere and ambition, but missing the coherence to truly deliver.

raw_payload.author: Blazehgehg
raw_payload.content:
Just a solid no-frills vampire movie. And by "no frills" I mean it does not feel too especially mired in vampire lore. Some of these movies get way into the weeds on what is true or false about vampires, like you'll have a big scene where a vampire grabs a crucifix and loudly proclaims, "THAT WON'T WORK ON ME." That kind of stuff.

Sinners has none of that. It assumes you know what's going on and kind of leaves it at that. It feels weird to say it, but that's surprisingly refreshing for a change.

Also wild to consider that special effects for an actor playing two versions of himself in the same scene have gotten so seamless and easy to do that I didn't even think about it here. Like, I know Michael B. Jordan doesn't have a twin brother, but it's so effortless and natural that I didn't even register it as anything. A totally invisible special effect (positive).

So it's a good vampire movie, but I especially love the epilogue. It's just a cool note to hang everything on.

raw_payload.author: tenshi_ippikiookami
raw_payload.content:
The beginning, slow, introducing 'thousands' of characters, sets perfectly the mood; the use of music in the plot is great; the actors do a great job (even if I am not of a fan of the need for two Michael B. Jordans (even if he is a great actor); the action is well done.

Not all is perfect, though, as the characters behave erratically, suiting the necessities of the plot more than behaving coherently. The change of pace can also be jarring. And even if the movie is more than two hours long, you might feel more advantage could've been taken from the amazing set up. But with so great music, so good all the elements (direction, acting, photography...), you will probably not care. Ryan Coogler has done a very good movie.

raw_payload.author: hello-33909
raw_payload.content: Watching Sinners was one of the most enjoyable film experiences I've had in years. The color grading was on another level, rich purples and deep umbers, that dreamy Portra 800 aesthetic was damn near perfect. Every frame looked like it belonged in a Magnum photo book. I watched on a high end TV in complete darkness, wife and kids outta the house, cold beer in hand and the sound system cranked to the absolute max. My only issue was that the vampires weren't really given a back story, which is maybe because they represented cultural appropriation or something like that.. idk, it's a minor issue and honestly I was too busy admiring the cinematography and color grading to even care.

raw_payload.author: makab-87460
raw_payload.content:
Throughout the movie, it needed to decide what it was trying to do. Had potential with the cast and some of the direction it seemed to be heading in but generally felt like it was trying to be a variety of different things at once.

Whilst I can commend a number of the performances for their commitment in their roles, it seemed to sway throughout between Dark horror and snazzy humour... But unfortunately frequently missed the mark and landed weakly.

The amount of random musical elements only made sense about half the time and I do have to question how much of that "Italian wine" The director had slurped before filming chunks of this movie.

I can respect it tried a have some sense of originality and it wasn't an absolutely terrible movie but just didn't live up to the hype in the end for me.

raw_payload.author: martijnpleijte
raw_payload.content: The movie has a super thin story... Metafores for black atrocities at that time... Wtf... It's not even visually spectacular!!! Like okay the actors aren't bad, but there is no character building almost... Like everything is super random and dumb, could be any survival horror film. Awful film . Don't waste your time!

raw_payload.author: logburner154
raw_payload.content: I'm going to keep this short It ain't a bad movie and I like the sets/ actin/ music. But as a horror movie it doesn't work. They haven't got the vampires right!! And that's a BIG part. The little segment with the Indians is under used and unfulfilled. A better and much less hyped movie is "Abraham Lincoln vampire slayer". They nailed that movie from start to finish. So if you want a genre mixing horror movie try Abraham Lincoln vampire slayer. You won't be disappointed. As for sinners. There are a few good touches but not enough to be memorable. Don't believe the hype. One more plus for the movie is the soundtrack. A nice job .

raw_payload.author: imdbfan-080401
raw_payload.content:
Let me be clear - Sinners is not just another vampire flick. If you walked away thinking this was just about bloodsuckers and fangs, congratulations, you've completely missed the point... and maybe that says more about you than the film.

This is not a horror movie - this is history dressed as horror. The vampires here are not monsters from fantasy; they are a razor-sharp metaphor for what has been happening for centuries: how white systems, white culture, have drained the soul, music, rhythm, and life out of Black communities. They enslaved bodies, yes - but they also sucked the art, the culture, the stories, the very spirit - repackaging it as their own, leaving emptiness behind.

Some people criticize films like this because they refuse to see beyond their own narrow lens. Maybe because it's uncomfortable. Maybe because deep down, they recognize themselves in the mirror - and we all know, vampires hate mirrors.

Yes, the vampires lure you in with a familiar hook - but Sinners flips the whole vampire mythos on its head to tell a much deeper, braver, and unapologetically Black story. And that's exactly why some people won't get it. It's not made for their comfort. It's made for truth.

Watch this film. But more importantly... see it.

raw_payload.author: Lois_lane18
raw_payload.content:
Absolute masterpiece. Sinners just gets it right. On the surface, the writing feels ordinary, maybe even simple, but the way it layers symbolism about imperialism, oppression, racism, and class divide hits different. Nothing feels forced. It breathes its elements through characters and settings.

Michael B. Jordan straight-up devoured this. Been a fan, but this might be his best work yet. You just forget it's the same guy playing both Stack and Smoke; the switch-ups in the body lang, accent, even emotional weight, this guy just pulls it off without missing a beat. The rest of the cast were just as locked in. Miles Caton, in his debut as Sammie, acts like he's been in the game for years. Hailee Steinfeld brings that quiet intensity, and Delroy Lindo gives it the weight only someone like him can carry. Everyone brought their A-game.

That juke joint music sequence though had no business going that hard. The vibes were unreal. Lights, crowd, the blend of cultures, rhythm, soul, the way the camera moved, everything collided in that room. And then "I Lied to You" hit, gave me goosebumps. I couldn't take my eyes off it even for a sec. Easily one of the coldest, most iconic sequences I've seen in years.

I'm not that technically sound when it comes to music, but that Irish undertone fading into Southern blues carried real weight, especially in the context of the film. Real pain, real soul. The lyrics felt lived in. Gotta give credit there.

First half gives low-key Western vibes, kinda simmering slow with Jim Crow-era undercurrents. Then it dives into a supernatural horror-musical groove in the second half without slipping even once.

Ryan Coogler cooked here. Original script, bold vision, visuals that just don't quit. Some shots had me frozen, just staring. Man really does not miss. Easily one of the best to come out this year. Highly recommended!!

raw_payload.author: vonavinavi
raw_payload.content: An acceptable film. It stole the basic plot of Rodriguez's film. Deus ex-machina solutions. Plot holes: centurion vampire with extreme bad luck. Vampires sometimes regenerate, sometimes don't. Sometimes irrational bloodthirsty vampire, sometimes not, etc. Apparently oral traditions only come from non-white tribal natives. Why the need to put ideological propaganda in a fantasy movie? Make a documentary instead for God's sake. First part interesting (Gecko brothers adventure), second part incongruous, inconsequential (vampires attacking the tavern). Some shots with a cinematic approach. A popcorn movie at best.

raw_payload.author: redraveniskira
raw_payload.content:
I genuinely regret not experiencing Sinners in theaters-this is hands down the most powerful and original films I've seen in years. Ryan Coogler delivers an ambitious vision of horror cinema that feels fresh, confident, and meticulously crafted. As both writer and director, Coogler masterfully balances atmosphere, emotion, and worldbuilding, creating a film setting that feels lived-in, eerie, and loaded with meaning.

The story is gripping from the first frame, with complex, well-drawn characters brought to life by a phenomenal cast. Michael B. Jordan gives a standout performance, further proving his ability to anchor a film with emotional depth and raw intensity. The synergy between Jordan and Coogler continues to be something special-they've become one of Hollywood's most compelling creative duos, rivaling the likes of Tom Cruise and Christopher McQuarrie in their consistency and boldness.

Visually, Sinners is a knockout. The cinematography is stunning-moody, elegant, and sometimes unsettling in the best ways. Every shot feels purposeful. The film's worldbuilding is another highlight: it doesn't just rely on tropes but builds its own mythology, blending supernatural horror with compelling themes to boot.

This isn't just a great horror movie-it's a cinematic masterpiece, period. Smart, original, and driven by real artistic vision, Sinners reminds us what cinema can do when it refuses to play it safe.
